pred_reason: The reentrancy vulnerability in changeOwner directly corresponds to SWC-107's improper state modification pattern, while the outdated compiler version (0.4.18) without built-in overflow protection aligns with SWC-101's integer overflow/underflow risks. Evidence: changeOwner updates balances before owner state (violating checks-effects-interactions); Solidity 0.4.18 lacks native overflow protection; Unsafe arithmetic operations bypass SafeMath protections

findings.Issue: Critical Reentrancy Vulnerability in changeOwner Function
findings.Description: The changeOwner function violates the checks-effects-interactions pattern by updating balances before updating the owner state variable, creating a reentrancy vulnerability. This issue appears in the smart contract code and was identified through manual analysis.
findings.Impact: Allows malicious contracts to re-enter and execute owner-only functions during ownership transfer, potentially leading to unlimited token minting, contract takeover, or fund loss

findings.Issue: Payable Constructor with No Withdrawal Mechanism
findings.Description: The constructor is marked payable but lacks ETH withdrawal functions, potentially locking any ETH sent to the contract. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: ETH sent to the contract during deployment or accidentally afterwards becomes permanently unrecoverable, resulting in permanent fund loss

findings.Description: The contract uses Solidity 0.4.18 which lacks built-in overflow protection and modern security features. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Relies entirely on SafeMath for arithmetic safety; any unprotected arithmetic operations could lead to overflow/underflow vulnerabilities, token manipulation, or fund loss

findings.Description: The changeOwner function performs direct balance assignments without SafeMath protection, inconsistent with other arithmetic operations in the contract. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Creates security inconsistency; if modified in future, could introduce arithmetic vulnerabilities; bypasses safety checks present in other functions

findings.Description: The burn function only allows the owner to burn their own tokens rather than tokens from any address, inconsistent with mint functionality. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Reduces owner's ability to manage token supply effectively; prevents removal of malicious tokens from other addresses; creates functional inconsistency

findings.Description: The owner has unlimited minting capability without supply caps, timelocks, or multi-sig requirements. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Allows owner to create unlimited tokens, potentially devaluing the token; enables rug pull scenarios; concentrates excessive power in single entity

findings.Description: The standard approve function is vulnerable to the ERC-20 race condition where spenders can use both old and new allowances. This issue appears in the smart contract code only.
findings.Impact: Allows malicious spenders to potentially spend more tokens than intended by exploiting transaction ordering; though mitigated by increase/decreaseApproval functions
